themeBlurb: Paper 2 Booklet A (Visual Text 5m) + Booklet B (Cloze 15m, Editing 10m, Synthesis & Transformation 10m, Open-Ended 20m). The biggest mark pool in the entire exam.

comprehension.title: Comprehension Open-Ended (20m)
comprehension.blurb: ONE passage (narrative or non-narrative, 500-600 words), ~10 questions. RACE framework on every 2+ mark question.
keyQuestions: Is this question literal, inferential, vocabulary-in-context, language-use, or personal response?
Does my answer Restate, Answer, Cite (with exact quote), and Explain?
For language-use: have I named the technique AND explained the effect?

glossary.definition: Restate, Answer, Cite Evidence, Explain. The standard structure for 2+ mark open-ended answers.
glossary.template: Use it on every inferential question.

glossary.definition: Asks 'how do you know' or 'what does this suggest'. Always supported with a text quote.
glossary.template: Marcus felt anxious; this is shown when 'his fingers trembled around the cup'.

glossary.definition: Asks about the effect of a phrase or technique. Answer in two parts: name the technique, explain the effect.
glossary.template: The simile 'as cold as ice' creates a sense of cruelty and distance.

comprehension.summaryHTML: Comprehension OE is 20 marks — one passage. The genre rotates between years (narrative or non-narrative). Question types: literal, inferential, vocabulary-in-context, language-use, author's purpose, personal response. Full strategy in Comprehension_Strategy_Guide.md.
pslePrompts: Always use RACE for 2+ mark questions.
Quote exactly — use quotation marks. Examiners reward exact citation.
For 'do you agree' questions: take a clear stance, then anchor in text evidence.

cloze.title: Comprehension Cloze (15m)
cloze.blurb: 15 blanks, one word each. Four clue types: grammar, context, collocation, register.
keyQuestions: What part of speech does this blank need?
What context clues in the surrounding sentences narrow the choice?
Is there a collocation that fits (e.g., 'make' + a noun)?

glossary.definition: A continuous passage with 15 blanks. Each blank takes exactly one word.
glossary.template: Use grammar + context + collocation clues together.

glossary.definition: Some blanks are decided by which word naturally pairs with surrounding words.
glossary.template: She made a _____ to volunteer. (decision) — verb 'make' + noun 'decision' is the natural pair.

cloze.summaryHTML: Comprehension Cloze is 15 marks — constructed-response (no options). 3–4 blanks per paper are decided by collocation knowledge alone. The Collocations bank (Collocations_KB.md) is the highest-leverage reference for this component.
pslePrompts: Read the whole passage once before filling any blank.
Check tense consistency across the whole passage.
Spell every answer correctly — half the cloze marks lose to spelling.

editing.title: Editing (10m)
editing.blurb: 10 lines with errors (~5 spelling, ~5 grammar). Some lines have NO error. Identify and correct.
keyQuestions: Is this line's error spelling or grammar?
If grammar, which of the six areas: tense, SVA, article, pronoun, adjective/adverb, preposition?
Am I sure my correction is spelled correctly?

glossary.definition: Subject-Verb Agreement. Singular subject → singular verb; plural → plural.
Common error: 'its' vs 'it's'. 'It's' = it is; 'its' = belonging to it.

editing.summaryHTML: Editing is 10 marks. Each item awards 1 mark only if BOTH the error is identified AND the correction is spelled right. Common errors: SVA, tense, article, pronoun, irregular plurals/past tense, double letters in spelling (occurred, embarrass).
pslePrompts: Read the whole passage first to catch context-dependent errors.
Don't change correct words — only correct what is wrong.
Double-check your correction's spelling before writing it.

synthesis.title: Synthesis & Transformation (10m)
synthesis.blurb: 5 items × 2 marks. Combine or transform sentences while preserving meaning. Patterns: conjunctions, relative clauses, conditionals, direct↔reported, active↔passive, inversion.
keyQuestions: Which transformation pattern is this — conjunction, relative clause, conditional, speech, voice, inversion?
Have I preserved the original meaning exactly?
Is my grammar perfect — tense, SVA, article all correct?

glossary.definition: Direct speech transformed to indirect: tense shifts back, pronouns change.
glossary.template: 'I am late,' she said. → She said that she _was_ late.

glossary.definition: Object becomes subject; agent introduced with 'by'.
glossary.template: The dog chased the cat. → The cat _was chased_ by the dog.

glossary.definition: Subject and auxiliary swap, used after 'Hardly', 'No sooner', 'Scarcely'.
glossary.template: Hardly had the bell rung when the students rushed out.

synthesis.summaryHTML: Synthesis & Transformation is 10 marks (5 items × 2). The highest density of grammar precision in the paper — one slip per item costs the full 2 marks. Drill the 8 high-frequency patterns to automaticity.
pslePrompts: Preserve the original meaning exactly — no extra information.
If 'Begin: Hardly had...' or 'Use: despite', start with the exact required word.
Despite + noun phrase; Although + clause. Never mix.

visual-text.title: Visual Text Comprehension (5m)
visual-text.blurb: 5 MCQ items on a poster, infographic, advertisement, or webpage. Tests literal, inferential, language-use, and purpose understanding.
keyQuestions: Is this question literal, inferential, language-use, or purpose?
Have I read ALL text including small print and captions?
Which option is most specifically supported by the visual?

glossary.definition: A wrong MCQ option that changes only one detail (a number, a name, a date). The most common trap.
Purpose = what the visual tries to DO; audience = who it speaks TO. Don't confuse them.
glossary.template: An NEA litter poster's purpose is to deter littering; its audience is the general public.

visual-text.summaryHTML: Visual Text is 5 marks (MCQ in Booklet A in the 2025+ format). See VisualText_Examples_KB.md for 6 worked Singaporean examples (HPB, NEA, LTA, SCDF, NLB, MOE).
pslePrompts: Read every word on the visual, including small print.
Compare each option word-for-word against the visual.
Block letters and bright colours are usually about urgency/attention — name the effect.
